item.title: Garnet texture and composition in the Makeng Fe skarn deposit, Southeastern China
item.dc:creator: Zihan Zhuang
Jing Xu
Wei Zheng
Taiping Zhao
Huochun Shen
Jiaxing Hu
Bo Xing
item.description: The fluid evolution and variation of physicochemical conditions during the skarn formation are fundamental to understanding skarn mineralization. Here, garnet from the Makeng Fe skarn deposit is investigated as a case study to constrain the fluids evolution and associated environments. Makeng deposit, a total identified iron resource of 434 Mt with an average grade of 38%, is the second largest Fe skarn deposit in Southeastern China. The deposit hosts multiple types of garnet (Grt I to Grt IV) occurring in distinct protolith settings, which are ranging from granite (endoskarn) through hornfel to marble (exoskarn). This study conducts a systematic investigation on garnet textures, major and trace elements, and U-Pb isotopes at Makeng. The results show that garnet is grossular-andradite series, exhibiting widespread oscillatory zoning (Adr 100 -Adr 20 ). The andradite-dominated (Adr 70∼100 ) component displays LREE-enriched chondrite-normalized patterns with positive Eu anomalies, suggesting crystallization from relatively oxidized fluids under higher water/rock ratios and more open-system conditions. In contrast, the grossular-dominated (Adr 20∼70 ) component is characterized by LREE depletion, indicating formation from relatively reduced fluids under lower water/rock ratios and more closed-system conditions. The U-Pb dating for garnet yield ages of 132.1 ± 2.7 Ma (n = 29) and 133.2 ± 1.6 Ma (n = 25), which are in general agreement with the crystallization ages of Dayang-Juzhou granite (132.6 ± 1.3 Ma) at Makeng mining area, indicating that magmatic-hydrothermal activity during this period was the main cause of Fe skarn mineralization. In addition, Makeng garnet is enriched in Sn (average 684 ppm) and W (average 133 ppm), with subordinate Ga (average 19 ppm) and Ge (average 36 ppm), reaching concentrations of up to several hundred ppm. The high contents of W and Sn probably indicate an affinity of W-Sn mineralization at Makeng and around, whereas the presence of Ga and Ge is due to their lithophile behavior and suggests a relative enrichment of these elements in source. This study takes Makeng as the case study to show that garnet could serves as an effective record of the skarn evolution and associated physicochemical conditions. Published: 2026-04-15T07:28:05+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.546 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zihan Zhuang; Jing Xu; Wei Zheng; Taiping Zhao; Huochun Shen; Jiaxing Hu; Bo Xing

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107098

Relevance: 0.546 (consider)

The fluid evolution and variation of physicochemical conditions during the skarn formation are fundamental to understanding skarn mineralization. Here, garnet from the Makeng Fe skarn deposit is investigated as a case study to constrain the fluids evolution and associated environments. Makeng deposit, a total identified iron resource of 434 Mt with an average grade of 38%, is the second largest Fe skarn deposit in Southeastern China. The deposit hosts multiple types of garnet (Grt I to Grt IV) occurring in distinct protolith settings, which are ranging from granite (endoskarn) through hornfel to marble (exoskarn). This study conducts a systematic investigation on garnet textures, major and trace elements, and U-Pb isotopes at Makeng. The results show that garnet is grossular-andradite series, exhibiting widespread oscillatory zoning (Adr 100 -Adr 20 ). The andradite-dominated (Adr 70∼100 ) component displays LREE-enriched chondrite-normalized patterns with positive Eu anomalies, suggesting crystallization from relatively oxidized fluids under higher water/rock ratios and more open-system conditions. In contrast, the grossular-dominated (Adr 20∼70 ) component is characterized by LREE depletion, indicating formation from relatively reduced fluids under lower water/rock ratios and more closed-system conditions. The U-Pb dating for garnet yield ages of 132.1 ± 2.7 Ma (n = 29) and 133.2 ± 1.6 Ma (n = 25), which are in general agreement with the crystallization ages of Dayang-Juzhou granite (132.6 ± 1.3 Ma) at Makeng mining area, indicating that magmatic-hydrothermal activity during this period was the main cause of Fe skarn mineralization. In addition, Makeng garnet is enriched in Sn (average 684 ppm) and W (average 133 ppm), with subordinate Ga (average 19 ppm) and Ge (average 36 ppm), reaching concentrations of up to several hundred ppm. The high contents of W and Sn probably indicate an affinity of W-Sn mineralization at Makeng and around, whereas the presence of Ga and Ge is due to their lithophile behavior and suggests a relative enrichment of these elements in source. This study takes Makeng as the case study to show that garnet could serves as an effective record of the skarn evolution and associated physicochemical conditions.

item.title: Quasi-rehealing of eastern North China craton by 0.9 Ga plume event
item.dc:creator: Xiangdong Su
Peng Peng
Zhuyin Chu
Peng Liou
Yanjie Tang
item.description: The cratonic roots, modified by mantle plume events, may undergo rehealing due to the presence of buoyant residues. However, whether the newly recovered mantle lithosphere can be as stable as the original one remains uncertain. Here, we report new data on trace elements and Sr–Nd–Hf–Os isotopes from the mafic rocks (920–906 Ma) associated with the Xuhuai plume event recorded in the eastern North China craton (NCC). Samples with Re–Os model ages (T MA ) of 890–960 Ma yield an isochron age of 933 ± 37 Ma (95 %-conf., MSWD=3.4) with an initial 187 Os/ 188 Os of 0.106 ± 0.080, indicating a depleted mantle plume source. These rocks typically experienced minimal crustal contamination but primarily assimilated contributions (up to 10 %) from the mantle lithosphere into the melts derived from the plume. The changing of trace element patterns of the rocks, illustrated by a transition from early tholeiites with OIB-affinity to later basalts resembling MORBs, indicates the thinning of the mantle lithosphere at ca. 900 Ma. However, the Re-depletion model age (T RD = 1.0–0.8 Ga) of mantle xenoliths from the eastern NCC infers lithospheric rehealing of the cratonic root following thinning, which likely driven by the accretion of buoyant residues from the Xuhuai plume. Nevertheless, the rehealed root, with a mosaic of mantle plume-derived residues formed through strain localization and cooling, is likely less stable compared to its precursor. Such instability stems from either insufficient mechanical strengthening within the mantle root or rheologically weak zones inherited from plume-lithosphere interaction. This quasi-rehealed cratonic root and its inherent weaknesses effectively preconditioned the Mesozoic lithospheric delamination beneath the eastern NCC. The study thus provides a framework to understand destabilization in other cratons where plume activity induced lithospheric quasi-rehealing over time. Published: 2026-04-16T12:49:59+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.544 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiangdong Su; Peng Peng; Zhuyin Chu; Peng Liou; Yanjie Tang

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120040

Relevance: 0.544 (consider)

The cratonic roots, modified by mantle plume events, may undergo rehealing due to the presence of buoyant residues. However, whether the newly recovered mantle lithosphere can be as stable as the original one remains uncertain. Here, we report new data on trace elements and Sr–Nd–Hf–Os isotopes from the mafic rocks (920–906 Ma) associated with the Xuhuai plume event recorded in the eastern North China craton (NCC). Samples with Re–Os model ages (T MA ) of 890–960 Ma yield an isochron age of 933 ± 37 Ma (95 %-conf., MSWD=3.4) with an initial 187 Os/ 188 Os of 0.106 ± 0.080, indicating a depleted mantle plume source. These rocks typically experienced minimal crustal contamination but primarily assimilated contributions (up to 10 %) from the mantle lithosphere into the melts derived from the plume. The changing of trace element patterns of the rocks, illustrated by a transition from early tholeiites with OIB-affinity to later basalts resembling MORBs, indicates the thinning of the mantle lithosphere at ca. 900 Ma. However, the Re-depletion model age (T RD = 1.0–0.8 Ga) of mantle xenoliths from the eastern NCC infers lithospheric rehealing of the cratonic root following thinning, which likely driven by the accretion of buoyant residues from the Xuhuai plume. Nevertheless, the rehealed root, with a mosaic of mantle plume-derived residues formed through strain localization and cooling, is likely less stable compared to its precursor. Such instability stems from either insufficient mechanical strengthening within the mantle root or rheologically weak zones inherited from plume-lithosphere interaction. This quasi-rehealed cratonic root and its inherent weaknesses effectively preconditioned the Mesozoic lithospheric delamination beneath the eastern NCC. The study thus provides a framework to understand destabilization in other cratons where plume activity induced lithospheric quasi-rehealing over time.

item.title: Temperature controls the episodic dynamics of deep slow slip
item.dc:creator: Zaccaria El Yousfi
Baptiste Rousset
Mathilde Radiguet
William B. Frank
item.prism:publicationName: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
item.description: The deformation regime that accommodates tectonic motion at plate boundaries changes as pressures and temperatures increase with depth, transitioning from the shallow frictional sliding of seismic ruptures to deep, viscous flow. We use recurring swarms of low-frequency earthquakes in this transition zone to measure the recurrence intervals and durations of accompanying slow slip across four plate boundaries. We find these time scales systematically decrease with depth and linearly scale with one another. Assuming a transition zone governed by episodic faulting, the observed time scales produce an average slow slip rate of 7 ± 2 mm/d across all four plate boundaries that is independent of depth. Thermal models place these slow slip dynamics within a common, narrow range of temperatures. Our results suggest deep slow slip is the result of the episodic unjamming of the plate boundary by frictional heterogeneities whose relaxation is modulated by surrounding temperature-dependent viscous material. Published: 2026-04-16T15:59:39+00:00 Venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences Score: 0.544 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zaccaria El Yousfi; Baptiste Rousset; Mathilde Radiguet; William B. Frank

Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

DOI: 10.1073/pnas.2524741123

Relevance: 0.544 (consider)

The deformation regime that accommodates tectonic motion at plate boundaries changes as pressures and temperatures increase with depth, transitioning from the shallow frictional sliding of seismic ruptures to deep, viscous flow. We use recurring swarms of low-frequency earthquakes in this transition zone to measure the recurrence intervals and durations of accompanying slow slip across four plate boundaries. We find these time scales systematically decrease with depth and linearly scale with one another. Assuming a transition zone governed by episodic faulting, the observed time scales produce an average slow slip rate of 7 ± 2 mm/d across all four plate boundaries that is independent of depth. Thermal models place these slow slip dynamics within a common, narrow range of temperatures. Our results suggest deep slow slip is the result of the episodic unjamming of the plate boundary by frictional heterogeneities whose relaxation is modulated by surrounding temperature-dependent viscous material.

item.title: Earth's Lower Mantle Predominated by Ferric Iron‐Rich Bridgmanite Inferred From High Pressure Elasticity Measurements
item.dc:creator: P. Saha
M. Murakami
N. Miyajima
item.description: Abstract The absence of sound‐velocity data spanning the entire lower mantle pressures for (Fe, Al)‐bearing bridgmanite impedes direct comparisons with seismic wave observations, leaving the chemistry of the lower mantle unresolved. The present ultra‐high pressure sound‐velocity measurements of in situ synthesized (Fe, Al)‐bearing bridgmanite up to 130 GPa indicate that a substantial portion of iron in it originally synthesized from a glass with ferrous (Fe 2+ ) iron becomes ferric (Fe 3+ ). Furthermore, its shear wave velocity profile shows a considerable reduction of approximately 1.6%–2.2% on average when compared to MgSiO 3 across the lower mantle. These results indicate that the lower mantle highly enriched with ferric iron‐rich bridgmanite comprising with greater than 95 vol% of the lower mantle, implying that the lower mantle is significantly rich in silica with a Mg/Si ratio approaching 1, compared to the upper mantle (Mg/Si ∼ 1.3), which supports the layered convection model securing the primordial chemical distinction. Published: 2026-04-17T12:25:56+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.543 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: P. Saha; M. Murakami; N. Miyajima

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl118109

Relevance: 0.543 (consider)

Abstract The absence of sound‐velocity data spanning the entire lower mantle pressures for (Fe, Al)‐bearing bridgmanite impedes direct comparisons with seismic wave observations, leaving the chemistry of the lower mantle unresolved. The present ultra‐high pressure sound‐velocity measurements of in situ synthesized (Fe, Al)‐bearing bridgmanite up to 130 GPa indicate that a substantial portion of iron in it originally synthesized from a glass with ferrous (Fe 2+ ) iron becomes ferric (Fe 3+ ). Furthermore, its shear wave velocity profile shows a considerable reduction of approximately 1.6%–2.2% on average when compared to MgSiO 3 across the lower mantle. These results indicate that the lower mantle highly enriched with ferric iron‐rich bridgmanite comprising with greater than 95 vol% of the lower mantle, implying that the lower mantle is significantly rich in silica with a Mg/Si ratio approaching 1, compared to the upper mantle (Mg/Si ∼ 1.3), which supports the layered convection model securing the primordial chemical distinction.

item.title: Spatial and Temporal Variations in Scheelite Composition Record Fluid Evolution Pathways in the Yongping Cu-W Skarn Deposit, South China
item.dc:creator: YunHao Ji
GuiQing Xie
Mathias Burisch
Xiaohui Chen
Xunjun Wang
Xiaoyu Shang
item.description: Abstract The trace element composition of scheelite has been widely used to constrain the temporal evolution of ore-forming fluids; however, its spatial compositional variations remain poorly understood. This study addresses this gap by integrating cathodoluminescence (CL) imaging, in situ U-Pb dating, trace element geochemistry, and Sr-Nd isotopic analyses of four types of scheelite from the proximal skarn and distal carbonate replacement deposit (CRD) ore bodies of the Yongping Cu-W skarn deposit, South China. U-Pb ages of scheelite from the proximal skarn and distal CRD ore bodies are 155.5 ± 3.4 Ma and 153.4 ± 1.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that W mineralization coincides with the emplacement of spatially associated Late Jurassic biotite granite porphyry. Scheelite trace element profiles across the deposit reveal decreasing Mo concentrations and Mo/Sr ratios, accompanied by increasing U, Sr, Re, and δEu from proximal to distal zones. We interpret that these compositional trends are influenced by progressive fluid-rock interaction, temperature, and pH along the fluid flow path. Correspondingly, scheelite from the proximal skarn ore exhibits lower 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.716261–0.720616), whereas scheelite from the distal CRD ore shows higher values (0.721162–0.723463), further supporting enhanced interaction between magmatic fluids and sedimentary wall rocks in distal settings. In contrast, initial ɛNd(t) values of scheelite range widely from –26.7 to –11.2 and do not correlate with the variation of 87Sr/86Sr ratios, suggesting that fluorite precipitation in proximal and distal locations may have modified Sm/Nd ratios and introduced Nd from the wall rock. These results demonstrate that the trace element and Sr isotopic composition of scheelite can serve as effective tracers of fluid evolution and flow direction, which may also be useful for exploration vectoring. Published: 2026-04-15T19:05:26+00:00 Venue: American Mineralogist Score: 0.540 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: YunHao Ji; GuiQing Xie; Mathias Burisch; Xiaohui Chen; Xunjun Wang; Xiaoyu Shang

Journal: American Mineralogist

DOI: 10.2138/am-2025-10000

Relevance: 0.540 (consider)

Abstract The trace element composition of scheelite has been widely used to constrain the temporal evolution of ore-forming fluids; however, its spatial compositional variations remain poorly understood. This study addresses this gap by integrating cathodoluminescence (CL) imaging, in situ U-Pb dating, trace element geochemistry, and Sr-Nd isotopic analyses of four types of scheelite from the proximal skarn and distal carbonate replacement deposit (CRD) ore bodies of the Yongping Cu-W skarn deposit, South China. U-Pb ages of scheelite from the proximal skarn and distal CRD ore bodies are 155.5 ± 3.4 Ma and 153.4 ± 1.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that W mineralization coincides with the emplacement of spatially associated Late Jurassic biotite granite porphyry. Scheelite trace element profiles across the deposit reveal decreasing Mo concentrations and Mo/Sr ratios, accompanied by increasing U, Sr, Re, and δEu from proximal to distal zones. We interpret that these compositional trends are influenced by progressive fluid-rock interaction, temperature, and pH along the fluid flow path. Correspondingly, scheelite from the proximal skarn ore exhibits lower 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.716261–0.720616), whereas scheelite from the distal CRD ore shows higher values (0.721162–0.723463), further supporting enhanced interaction between magmatic fluids and sedimentary wall rocks in distal settings. In contrast, initial ɛNd(t) values of scheelite range widely from –26.7 to –11.2 and do not correlate with the variation of 87Sr/86Sr ratios, suggesting that fluorite precipitation in proximal and distal locations may have modified Sm/Nd ratios and introduced Nd from the wall rock. These results demonstrate that the trace element and Sr isotopic composition of scheelite can serve as effective tracers of fluid evolution and flow direction, which may also be useful for exploration vectoring.

item.title: Tracking Deep Magma Migration Through Diffuse CO <sub>2</sub> Degassing: Insights From Piton de la Fournaise Volcano (2013–2023)
item.dc:creator: B. Bénard
A. Di Muro
A. Finizola
A. Peltier
V. Ferrazzini
I. Vlastelic
P. Besson
V. Famin
R. Gusset
K. Mussard
P. Kowalski
P. Boissier
C. Brunet
F. Lauret
N. Desfete
C. Griot
M. Liuzzo
S. Gurrieri
G. Giudice
F. Grassa
M. Longo
item.description: Abstract Volcano monitoring requires identifying and distinguishing physico‐chemical processes at various depths within magma plumbing systems and their links to volcanic activity. Long‐term monitoring of deep magma reservoirs, extending down to tens of kilometers, is crucial even though signals from these depths are often altered or attenuated before reaching the surface. Here, we investigate the architecture and dynamics of the Piton de la Fournaise plumbing system by integrating soil CO 2 flux mapping, continuous soil CO 2 monitoring, seismicity, ground deformation, and lava geochemistry recorded from 2013 to 2023. Environmental influences on CO 2 time series are corrected using advanced cross‐correlation techniques. The system's architecture is mapped by combining CO 2 flux data, self‐potential mapping, and seismic density analysis. Results confirm a complex plumbing system, laterally shifted relative to the summit, with a shallow reservoir (∼2 km) near sea level fed by melts from the Moho (∼10 km), branching along the N120 rift zone and extending to a deeper melt zone at ∼30 km depth. Periodic recharge of the Moho deep reservoir induces intermittent CO 2 degassing in soils and feeds a hydrothermal system. Two major recharge events are identified: (a) in 2013–2015, involving seismic activity, soil CO 2 increase, inflation, and initial eruptions; and (b) in 2019, marked by rapid magma transfer, degassing, and eruptions with distinct strontium isotopic signatures. These findings highlight the value of diffuse CO 2 monitoring for understanding deep volcanic processes and provide early indicators of magma recharge, improving volcanic hazard assessment for basaltic shield volcanoes like Piton de la Fournaise. Published: 2026-04-13T18:06:28+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.534 (ignore)

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012600

Relevance: 0.534 (ignore)

Abstract Volcano monitoring requires identifying and distinguishing physico‐chemical processes at various depths within magma plumbing systems and their links to volcanic activity. Long‐term monitoring of deep magma reservoirs, extending down to tens of kilometers, is crucial even though signals from these depths are often altered or attenuated before reaching the surface. Here, we investigate the architecture and dynamics of the Piton de la Fournaise plumbing system by integrating soil CO 2 flux mapping, continuous soil CO 2 monitoring, seismicity, ground deformation, and lava geochemistry recorded from 2013 to 2023. Environmental influences on CO 2 time series are corrected using advanced cross‐correlation techniques. The system's architecture is mapped by combining CO 2 flux data, self‐potential mapping, and seismic density analysis. Results confirm a complex plumbing system, laterally shifted relative to the summit, with a shallow reservoir (∼2 km) near sea level fed by melts from the Moho (∼10 km), branching along the N120 rift zone and extending to a deeper melt zone at ∼30 km depth. Periodic recharge of the Moho deep reservoir induces intermittent CO 2 degassing in soils and feeds a hydrothermal system. Two major recharge events are identified: (a) in 2013–2015, involving seismic activity, soil CO 2 increase, inflation, and initial eruptions; and (b) in 2019, marked by rapid magma transfer, degassing, and eruptions with distinct strontium isotopic signatures. These findings highlight the value of diffuse CO 2 monitoring for understanding deep volcanic processes and provide early indicators of magma recharge, improving volcanic hazard assessment for basaltic shield volcanoes like Piton de la Fournaise.
